coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots derived from biomass of Polyalthia bullata root extract as colorimetric sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ ions

coredata.dc:description:
In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 15 h using a biomass from the root extract of Polyalthia bullata as a carbon source. The synthesis process did not involve any chemical reagents or surface passivation. The calculated quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs in comparison with a quinine sulfate was 7.55 %. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that the CDs possessed a spherical shape and narrow size distribution with an average size of ∼3.34 ± 0.76 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter was 39.55 nm using the DLS technique with a PDI value of 0.206. UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed two absorption peaks at 252 nm and 324 nm which attributed to
π -
π
∗
transition of C=C bonds and
n -
π
∗
transition of C=O bonds in CDs, respectively. The CDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence (FL) with excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The CDs showed excitation-dependent FL emission spectra upon excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm with a blue shift. The average FL lifetime of the CDs was 5.634 ns. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) identified numerous functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. The CDs were employed as a nanoprobe for Fe3+ ions detection using a colorimetric approach by measuring absorbance at 200–700 nm. The sensitivity of the synthesized CDs towards Fe3+ ions was examined at concentrations from 2.5 to 200 µM. Within the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–57 µM, an excellent linear relationship with coefficient of determination (
R
2
) of 0.9926 was observed, yielding a limit of detection of 0.186 µM. The detection method demonstrated satisfactory recovery rates when tested on real water samples such as lake, farm, and tap water.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge funding from Malaysian Ministry of Higher Education under MTUN Matching Grant with grant number RDU1901112 & UIC211503 and Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah (UMPSA) International Publication Grant with grant number RDU213307 .

Dr. Muna E. Raypah received the B.Sc. degree (Hons.) in Physics from Hodeida University, Al-Hodeida, Yemen, in 2003, and the M.Sc. degree (Hons.) in Solid-State Physics from the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia, in 2013. She was a Graduate Assistant at the Universiti of Science Malaysia from October 2014 to May 2015. From June 2015 to May 2018, she received a Ph.D. Fellowship Award from the Universiti of Science Malaysia and completed her Ph.D. degree (Hons.) in thermal processes in devices and packaging from the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia, in 2020. From July 2021 to June 2023, she served as a Postdoctoral Fellow in the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia. Since August 2023, she has been a Postdoctoral Fellow at Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah, Gambang, Malaysia. Her research interests include materials characterization, carbon quantum dots, optical devices, and chemometrics.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots derived from biomass of Polyalthia bullata root extract as colorimetric sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 15 h using a biomass from the root extract of Polyalthia bullata as a carbon source. The synthesis process did not involve any chemical reagents or surface passivation. The calculated quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs in comparison with a quinine sulfate was 7.55 %. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that the CDs possessed a spherical shape and narrow size distribution with an average size of ∼3.34 ± 0.76 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter was 39.55 nm using the DLS technique with a PDI value of 0.206. UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed two absorption peaks at 252 nm and 324 nm which attributed to
π -
π
∗
transition of C=C bonds and
n -
π
∗
transition of C=O bonds in CDs, respectively. The CDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence (FL) with excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The CDs showed excitation-dependent FL emission spectra upon excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm with a blue shift. The average FL lifetime of the CDs was 5.634 ns. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) identified numerous functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. The CDs were employed as a nanoprobe for Fe3+ ions detection using a colorimetric approach by measuring absorbance at 200–700 nm. The sensitivity of the synthesized CDs towards Fe3+ ions was examined at concentrations from 2.5 to 200 µM. Within the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–57 µM, an excellent linear relationship with coefficient of determination (
R
2
) of 0.9926 was observed, yielding a limit of detection of 0.186 µM. The detection method demonstrated satisfactory recovery rates when tested on real water samples such as lake, farm, and tap water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon-derived fluorescent nanostructures known as carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their nanoscale size (less than 10 nm). Extensive research has explored their diverse applications in biomedicine, optoelectronics, photocatalysis, sensing, and light-emitting devices. This interest is driven by their exceptional properties, including strong luminescence, water solubility, biocompatibility, stability, and low toxicity. Numerous techniques have been suggested to synthesize CDs including the pyrolysis carbonization [1], chemical oxidation [2], microwave [3], laser ablation [4], ultrasonic treatment [5], and hydrothermal [6] methods. However, the utilization of costly precursors, hazardous chemicals, or complex processes limits the sustainability and scalability of several current synthesis techniques. Furthermore, most of these methods require specific tools and challenging operating conditions, such as high temperature and pressure or radiation environments. This may raise concerns about safety and result in significant energy waste, which will restrict future applications and the potential for CDs production in industrial operations. The hydrothermal method is a particularly effective approach for synthesizing CDs compared to other widely used methods because it combines eco-friendliness, simplicity, non-toxicity, and affordability.

Industrial and domestic waste are major contributors to the pollution of water resources such as rivers, lakes, and oceans. Therefore, there is a pressing need for effective methods to identify and treat these pollutants. In recent years, there has been growing interest in utilizing CDs for the detection of environmental pollutants, particularly metal ions such as Fe2+ and Fe3+ [7], Ag+ [8], Pb2+ [9], Cu2+ [10], Cr3+ and Cr6+ [11], and Hg+ [12]. Elevations of Fe3+ in environmental water have become serious health risks for humans since an excess of this mineral can cause anemia, liver damage, heart disease, and renal failure. This raised the demand for sensitive, affordable, and selective sensors for detecting Fe3+. The CDs-based fluorescence approach has gathered a lot of attention due to its ease of use, speed, and excellent sensitivity for Fe3+ detection. Given their strong affinity, Fe3+ can be linked to the CDs surface functional groups, causing the quenching of the fluorescence intensity.

Carbon sources including biomass and waste materials can be used to easily produce CDs without the need for costly/hazardous chemicals or labour-intensive post-treatment processes. Numerous studies described the synthesis of CDs from biomass/waste to detect Fe3+ ions utilizing the fluorometric approach. According to Li et al. [13], CDs are used as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ by synthesizing them from discarded leaves of camphor trees using a hydrothermal method. CDs were produced from arabica coffee waste by Nazar et al. [3] using microwave heating to detect Fe3+. Tang et al. [14] obtained CDs from a waste of camellia fruit shells through a hydrothermal technique for Fe3+ detection. These investigations utilized the fluorometric method to detect Fe3+, which depends on the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs. The fluorescence quenching method relies on the reduction in fluorescence intensity caused by the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ ions. This method generally demands more expensive equipment, such as fluorescence spectrometers, making it a higher-cost choice. On the other hand, colorimetric sensors have garnered lots of attention because of their affordability, effortlessness of usage, rapid analysis, and visibility. The colorimetric method offers distinct advantages over the fluorometric approach in its simplicity and ease of operation, as it doesn't require sophisticated equipment to obtain results [15]. It requires basic tools, such as a UV–Vis spectrophotometer or even visual inspection. Using this method, the interaction between CDs and Fe3+ ions alters their absorbance characteristics, resulting in observable color changes or spectral changes/shifts. In the colorimetric technique, the complexes formed absorb UV/Vis light at a particular wavelength and this absorption is proportional to concentration of metal ions [16]. Additionally, this method relied on electronic transitions (
π -
π
∗
and
n -
π
∗
) that offer fundamental insights into the interaction of Fe3+ with CDs. Qui et al. [17] synthesized CDs from L-histidine and ethylene imine polymer for detecting Co2+. Excellent linearity was shown in 0–200 µM range by both the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy modes, with LODs of 1.0023 μM and 0.75 μM, respectively. Lu et al. [8] produced CDs to identify Ag+ using poly(2-ethyl-2-oxazoline) and 1,2,4,5-benzene tetramine tetrahydrochloride. By fluorescence and absorbance methods, the LOD was determined to be 0.05 μM and 0.46 μM at doses of 10–180 μM and 0–8 μM, respectively. Boobalan et al. [9] used a hydrothermal technique to synthesize CDs from oyster mushrooms, which were then used as a colorimetric sensor to identify Pb2+ ions. Li et al. [18] utilized the absorbance technique for identifying Ag+ with LOD of 0.13 µM at 1.0–100 µM using gold nanoparticles.

Growing global consciousness about health and wellness has focused emphasis on plant-based materials as therapeutic and preventive agents that are less toxic and have less adverse effects than manmade medicines. The woody medicinal plant Polyalthia bullata (P. bullata) king, often referred to as Tongkat Ali Hitam in Malaysia belongs to the Annonaceae family and genus Polyalthia [19]. This tree or shrub is predominantly found in moist tropical environments and has traditionally been used as an herbal aphrodisiac in Malaysia and Southeast Asia. Reports indicated that extracts from this plant possess various pharmacological properties, including antibacterial, aphrodisiac, cytotoxic, and antioxidant activities.

P. bullata contains alkaloids and flavonoids that can enhance its healing attributes. P. bullata root is widely recognized for its antibacterial, antioxidant, and aphrodisiac benefits. The root of P. bullata is a suitable material for producing CDs due to its rich organic composition, involving abundant carbon-based compounds such as lignin, cellulose, and other polyphenolic substances. In addition, P. bullata roots contain bioactive compounds such as alkaloids and flavonoids. Alkaloids, being nitrogenous compounds, provide a rich source of carbon and nitrogen for the synthesis of CDs, enhancing their optical and electronic properties. Flavonoids, which contain aromatic rings, facilitate the formation of graphitic or sp2 carbon domains within CDs, contributing to fluorescence and stability. Both alkaloids and flavonoids carry various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and functionalization of CDs, enabling specific interactions with target analytes like Fe3+ ions. This study aimed to synthesize carbon dots from the waste biomass of P. bullata root extract as a colorimetric sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The biomass of P.bullata was generated in an organic laboratory during an extraction process that involved the use of five organic solvents with varying polarities, namely n-hexane, dichloromethane, chloroform, ethyl estate, and methanol, that were used to extract the powdered root of P. bullata in order to investigate and validate its traditional and pharmacological uses. The aqueous solution obtained after the extraction of the root powder of P. bullata biomass was utilized as the carbon source which was hydrothermally treated to attain the CDs. To the best of our knowledge, the biomass of Polyalthia bullata root extraction has not been previously reported as a precursor for CDs synthesis. This novel approach demonstrates the potential of biomass resources for nanomaterial development. Unlike several reported methods, the synthesis in this study did not involve chemical reagents, doping, or surface passivation, ensuring a greener approach to the production of CDs. The resulting CDs have excellent water solubility, high stability, and remarkable fluorescence features. The obtained CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques. The obtained CDs were used as a colorimetric sensor to selectively detect Fe3+ with a strong linear relation (0–57 µM) and LOD of 0.186 µM. Acceptable recoveries were achieved when the CDs sensor was used to effectively detect Fe3+ ions in lake, farm, and tap water. These results point to the considerable promise of CDs as a novel probe for the reliable, sensitive, and visible recognition of Fe3+ ions and possible applications in real environments. By leveraging waste biomass and a single-step, reagent-free synthesis, the hydrothermal process positions itself as a superior approach for sustainable nanomaterial development, particularly for applications like heavy metals sensing and environmental monitoring.

The roots of P. bullata plant were purchased from the herbal company (Bodywell Sdn Bhd, Malaysia). The biomass of P. bullata root extraction was obtained from an organic laboratory at the Faculty of Industrial Science and Technology, University Malaysia Pahang Al-sultan Abdullah (UMPSA). Ultrapure water with 18.0 MΩ (Millipore) was utilized throughout the experiments. Quinine sulfate dihydrate was acquired from Fisher Scientific Sdn Bhd, Malaysia. FeCl3, NiSO4·6H2O, LiCl, ZnSO4·7H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, NaNO3, CH3CO2K, MgSO4, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, AgNO3, Pb(C2H3O2)2, SnCl2·2H2O, and CaCO3 were obtained from R&M Chemicals and Sigma Aldrich.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were attained from an FEI Talos L120C electron microscope (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by an X′Pert3 powder diffractometer (PANalytical Ltd., Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). A PHI VersaProbe 4 equipment (Physical Electronics, USA) was utilized for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) examination, with monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) serving as the X-ray source. UV–Vis-NIR Spectrometer LAMBDA 1050 (PerkinElmer, UK) was used to obtain UV–Vis absorption spectra. Fluorescence (FL) spectra and lifetime were measured using FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) equipped with an Xe lamp as a light source. The instrument response function (IRF) and the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique were employed to assess the FL lifetime. Zetasizer Nano S90 and dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology (Malvern, UK) were used to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs.

200 g of the root powder of P. bullata was extracted with 1500 mL of methanol: water (3:1) for two cycles using ultrasonic extraction for 60 min. The mixture was separated using filter paper and the filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure until the volume reached one-third of the initial volume. Fig. 1 represents the schematic diagram of the carbon dots synthesis procedure. 20 mL of the biomass extract of P. bullata root was mixed with 10 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 5 min. The mixture was moved to 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 15 h in an air oven. The product was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. The dark-orange solution with a pH of 5.85 was obtained after filtering using a filter paper and centrifuging twice for 30 min at 4500 rpm each. Two to three rounds of dichloromethane (DCM) washings were performed on the filtered solution. The process of separation during DCM washing (Fig. 1) produced two layers: an aqueous layer at the top of the separatory funnel (retained solution) and an organic layer at the bottom (discarded solution). To remove any remaining DCM, the solution was allowed to sit in a fume hood for the entire night. Subsequently, the solution was mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water, sonicated for 5 min, centrifuged for 20 min at 4500 rpm, and filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter. The final solution was condensed using the rotary evaporator set at 50 °C. The concentrated solution (< 15 mL) was purified via column chromatography with silica gel as a stationary phase and 70 % methanol: 30 % ethyl acetate as a mobile phase. The purified solution was left in a fume hood overnight. The solution was then centrifuged for 15 min at 3000 rpm and filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to obtain CDs with a clear color. The final solution was refrigerated at 4 °C for a week while the sediment and turbidity were monitored. Finally, the powder of the CDs was obtained using a freeze dryer for 5 days.

100 µL of purified CDs solution was diluted using 70 mL ultrapure water to make a stock solution. A stock solution of 1.0 mM concentration from each metal ion including Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Sn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, and Fe3+ was prepared using ultrapure water. To investigate the detection of individual metal ions, 3 mL of CDs was mixed with 600 µL of each metal ion from the stock. The mixture was mixed thoroughly using a vortex for 30 sec followed by incubation for 15 min at room temperature. The concentration of each metal was so determined to be roughly 167 µM. The absorbance of the CDs (blank) and the CDs mixed with each metal ion (CDs + metal ion) were recorded. To determine the selectivity of the CDs to Fe3+, 3 mL of the CDs with 300 µL of each metal and 300 µL of Fe3+ were mixed separately. The absorption spectra were performed after 30 sec of vortexing and 15 min of incubation of the mixture (CDs + Fe3+ + metal ion). To assess the sensitivity of the CDs to Fe3+ ions, the solutions of CDs and Fe3+ with various concentrations of Fe3+ (2.5–200 µM) were prepared. The applicability of the synthesized CDs for detecting Fe3+ ions in real environmental samples was evaluated. Three samples from KotaSAS Lake (Kuantan, Malaysia), a local farm (Kuantan, Malaysia), and tap water from our laboratory were collected. The samples were filtered using a 0.22 µm filter to eliminate large particles and particulate matters. Various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (5, 10, 15, and 50 μM) were used to prepare spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups on the surface of the CDs are recognized using the FTIR spectrum as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The presence of hydroxyl groups (–OH) is indicated by the large peak at 3317.60 cm−1, which is typically associated with the O–H stretching vibration. The peak located at 1600.53 cm−1 is linked to C=C stretching vibrations of aromatic rings or conjugated double bonds. It signifies the existence of sp2 hybridized carbon structures, like those in aromatic rings or graphitic domains. The bending vibrations of O–H groups or the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds in alkanes are responsible for the peak at 1408.46 cm−1. A prominent peak at 1096.75 cm−1 implies the existence of ether or alcohol groups and is indicative of the tensile vibration of C–O [20]. The remaining peaks located at 644.13, 657.61, 616.60 cm−1, and so on are frequently linked to C–O bending vibrations and complex modes that include numerous bonds and atoms. The existence of the functional groups reveals that the surface of the CDs is highly functionalized, which may contribute to their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solution.

XPS survey spectrum presented in Fig. 2b indicates the presence of C, O, and Ca, with corresponding binding energies of 290.2 eV, 537.2 eV, and 352.6 eV. The atomic ratios of C, O, and Ca were 56.28 %, 41.01 %, and 2.71 %, respectively. Durrani et al. [21] and Sharma et al. [22] claimed the presence of Ca in the XPS pattern of the carbon dots synthesized from the roots of Salvadora persica plant (6.30 %) and Brassica flowers (3.48 %), respectively. A high concentration of carbon and oxygen in XPS demonstrates the existence of functionalization groups on the surface of CDs, including carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups. In Fig. 2c, five sub-peaks with centres at 287.3, 288.9, 290.2, 291.8, and 293.6 eV were discernible upon deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum. The carbon in these peaks can be attributed to species C=O/C–O–C, C–C=O/–COOH, C–C, O–C=O, and C=C [23–26]. The peaks at 290.2 eV, 291.8 eV, and 293.5 eV provide evidence of aromatic structures of synthesized CDs. The carbon in highly oxidized species or more complex oxygen-containing groups could result in a shift in the binding energy and may be linked to peaks in 290–295 eV. The O 1s deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 2d showed the existence of four sub-peaks located at 529.4, 534.5, 537.2, and 540.7 eV. These peaks linked to C=O, C–O, adsorbed water, and adsorbed oxygen/water groups, respectively [27]. The peaks at 529.4 eV and 534.5 eV suggest oxygen in functional groups such as carbonyls, hydroxyls, and ethers, which are common in CDs. The analyses of the FTIR and XPS spectra accord with each other.

The size distribution and morphology of the CDs that were acquired from TEM images are displayed in Fig. 3 a. It is noticed that the CDs have a spherical shape and single-dispersity nanoparticles with a rather limited size distribution (see the inset on the above-right). The size distribution histogram presented in Fig. 3b denotes that the CDs have an average particle size of 3.34 ± 0.74 nm and fall primarily within 2.0 to 5.0 nm range. Using the DLS approach, the hydrodynamic diameter was 39.55 nm, with a polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.206 (Fig. 3c). The value of PDI suggests that while there is considerable diversity in the particle sizes, they are generally more uniform. Moreover, it is noteworthy that the dimensions of nanoparticles as determined by TEM in vacuum are less than their hydrodynamic diameter as determined by DLS in a solvent [28,29]. As seen in Fig. 3d, a combination of amorphous and crystalline phases is suggested by the various peaks in the XRD patterns. The graphitic properties that are frequently detected in CDs are displayed by the peak at 21.52°, which corresponds to the graphite (002) plane with a d-spacing of 0.41 nm. The larger interlayer spacing of prepared CDs compared to bulk graphite (0.34 nm) might be attributed to the minor expansion between carbon layers. This could also be linked to the distortion of the carbon lattice plane caused by heteroatoms which induce structural defects in CDs [30]. The degree of graphitization is correlated with the formation of a stronger diffraction peak at 31.14° and trivial peaks at 28.19°, 38.19°, and 55.42°. Furthermore, the observed minor peak at 43.94° can be related to Ca (220) plane [22]. The peak at 28.19° can be associated with plane (002) of graphitic domains and represent graphitic or sp2 hybridized carbon clusters within CDs [31]. The peak at 38.19° may be the result of short-range order or partial graphitization in amorphous carbon materials. This reveals a partly disordered carbon structure, which is explained by the presence of functional groups that contain oxygen on the surface of CDs [32]. Sharp diffraction peak is indicative of better CDs crystallinity [33]. The sharp peak at 31.14° links to the crystal plane (100) and suggests a crystalline phase. The d-spacing values calculated for the (002), (100), and (101) planes at 28.19°, 31.14°, and 38.19°, respectively, are 0.32 nm, 0.29 nm, and 0.24 nm, closely matching the lattice spacing of graphitic carbon.

The optical properties of CDs were explored by analyzing their UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs. It is evident that there are two peaks at 252 nm and 324 nm, which link to
π -
π
∗
and
n -
π
∗
of the transitions C=C and C=O, respectively [34]. Additionally, Fig. 4a indicates that the maximum excitation wavelength is 378 nm for the excitation scan and the maximum emission wavelength is 472 nm for the emission scan. In inset Fig. 4a, the images of the solution of CDs reveal a pale-yellow solution in daylight with intense blue light when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. A chromaticity diagram of CIE 1931 color space was established from the FL spectrum of the CDs at
λ
ex
=
380 nm. Fig. 4b shows that the emission is displayed by the CDs in (0.182, 0.180) color coordinates. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs were attained at excitation wavelengths 300–500 nm. As can be shown, the CDs have an optimum excitation wavelength of 380 nm, which corresponds to an emission wavelength of 423 nm. Additionally, the maximum emission peak shifts (inset of Fig. 4c) from a longer wavelength (481 nm at
λ
ex
=
300 nm) to a shorter wavelength (392 nm at
λ
ex
=
500 nm) and the FL intensity dramatically drops. Furthermore, the wide emission peak implies that the sample contains a variety of emitting species which could be associated with surface states, a wide range of sizes, and fluorescent molecular moieties [35]. The excitation-dependent FL behavior shown in Fig. 4c is a common feature in the CDs and this performance is consistent with the earlier reports on CDs [35,36].

τ

) [37,38] as follows:

, suggesting the presence of numerous radiative species. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the calculated

was 5.634 ns which is connected to the intrinsic surface states and in agreement with earlier reports on the CDs [39].

The quantum yield (
QY
) of the CDs was estimated using a reference fluorophore which was a quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with
QY
of 54 %. A concentration of 5 µg/mL was prepared by diluting both quinine sulfate and CDs. This concentration was chosen since the absorbance (or optical density) at

= 380
nm was <0.1 to avoid the re-absorption effect. Based on a comparison with quinine sulfate, the
QY
of the CDs was estimated using the following formula [40]:

where I,

was 7.55 %, which is either greater or comparable to the earlier studies [8,14,41].

To assess the stability of the absorbance of the CDs, a range of variables and conditions were examined including pH, ionic strength, temperature, continuous UV exposure, and duration of storage. The CDs suspension exhibited excellent stability with no turbidity or sedimentation even after six months of air storage at room temperature. Fig. 5 a displays that the CDs show remarkable stability in NaCl solution, even at concentrations as high as 1.0 M. Fig. 5b illustrates the relationship between the pH and absorbance intensity. When the pH was adjusted from 1.71 to 12.31, there was an increase in absorbance intensity, which indicates that the CDs have significant pH-dependent behavior and are sensitive to both acidic and basic environments. The changes in pH that affect the absorbance of the CDs might cause variations in the electronic transitions of
π -
π
∗
and
n -
π
∗
in graphite nanodomains by replenishing or depleting their valence bands, which means changes in surface charge brought about by acidification and alkalinity [42]. Fig. 5c exhibits no change in the absorbance even after continuous exposure to UV radiation at a wavelength of 254 nm for a duration of 120 min. Fig. 5d confirms that CDs are stable at high temperatures since the absorbance does not change at temperatures >100 °C. Therefore, the produced CDs have good absorbance stability under various conditions, making them ideal colorimetric probes with significant application potential. Further, the fluorescence stability of the synthesized CDs was examined through continuous irradiation at 365 nm for 5 h. Fig. 6 illustrates the photostability of CDs solution before and after exposure to 365 nm radiation continuously for 5 h. Notably, irradiated CDs show a relatively slight variation in the fluorescence intensity compared to non-irradiated CDs, confirming the remarkable photostability of obtained CDs [43,44].

An assessment was conducted on the effect of several cations (at concentration 167 µM) on the absorbance intensity of the synthesized CDs. These cations included Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Sn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, and Fe3+. As seen in Fig. 7 a, the presence of Fe3+ ions results in a significantly enhanced absorbance, whereas the absorbance of the other metal ions either remains the same or changes slightly when compared to the blank CDs (see Fig. 7b). This proves that Fe3+ ions have a high selectivity because the functional groups on the CDs can readily couple with Fe3+ ions. Moreover, this finding reveals that CDs have a strong selective affinity for Fe3+ ions, suggesting that they could be effectively employed as an absorbance sensor for Fe3+ detection. Interestingly, an immediate change in the color from pale yellow to dark gray was observed in the CDs solution upon the addition of Fe3+ ions (inset in Fig. 7a). This is probably due to a combination of processes, such as complexation, quenching, particle aggregation, and charge transfer.

To examine potential interferences and determine the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+, a solution of Fe3+ with 83 µM was mixed with a solution of CDs that included other ions at 83 µM concentration. As shown in Fig. 8 , the CDs exhibited excellent selectivity for identifying Fe3+ ions even when extra ions were present. The strong selectivity of the CDs for Fe3+ is mainly due to photoelectron transfer, which was facilitated by the high-affinity interaction between Fe3+ and the –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of the CDs [20]. Furthermore, the strong selectivity for Fe3+ may be the consequence of nonradiative electron-hole recombination which causes the absorbance enhancement (FL quenching). This process occurs when electrons from the excited CDs are transferred to the empty orbital of Fe3+ [45]. Thus, the synthesized CDs in this study offer a practical alternative as a sensor for sensing Fe3+ ions.

Various Fe3+ concentrations (2.5–200 µM) were introduced to the CDs solution in order to determine the sensitivity of the CDs to Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. 9 a, the absorbance intensity of the CDs increased progressively with an increase in Fe3+ concentration indicating that the CDs and Fe3+ ions interact in a dose-dependent manner. Apparently, the absorbance spectra revealed an increase rather than forming a new absorption peak. The primary basis for the detection process is the interaction of Fe3+ ions with the surface of CDs, which disrupts their electronic transitions and causes detectable variations in absorbance. As can be seen, the most notable changes occur in the UV region associated with
π -
π
∗
transitions, which are linked to the aromatic or conjugated structures in the CDs. The abundant functional groups on the CDs can serve as ligands for Fe3+ ions. As a result, the electronic structure of CDs is altered which affects their absorption and emission characteristics. The development of the nonfluorescent CDs + Fe3+ ground state complex, which caused the fluorescence to quench is one explanation for this change in the absorption spectrum [46]. The results reported by Yang et al. [47] suggested that the functional groups on the CDs may have a special ability to coordinate with Fe3+ and the strong intermolecular interactions may lead to CDs aggregation as the concentration rises. Hence, we infer that the suggested Fe3+ system caused CDs aggregation, which may increase the absorption intensity. Furthermore, Fe3+ ions are strong electron acceptors due to their high oxidation potential and partially filled d-orbital [45]. Therefore, the electron transfer from the CDs to Fe3+ may occur, altering the electronic environment of the CDs and leading to increased absorption.

Fig. 9b illustrates the relationship between
A /
A 0
and the concentration of Fe3+. The absorbance responses to the various concentrations of Fe3+ ions in Fig. 9b are similar to the fluorescence responses to concentrations of Cu2+ in [48], Ag+ in [8], and Fe3+ in [13]. In the inset, a robust linear relation was found in the range 0–57 µM with
R
2
of 0.9926. The linear equation was
A /
A 0
= 0.0213 C + 1.0002
, where C is the concentration of Fe3+ ions.

A limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as follows:
(3)
LOD =
3 δ
S
where δ is the standard deviation of blank sample and S is the slope of the linear fit. The LOD was found to be 0.186 µM, which is lower than the maximum permitted level (5.36 µM) of Fe3+ in drinking water specified by the World Health Organization (WHO). According to the results, the CDs showed a high degree of sensitivity to Fe3+. Table 1 presents the comparison between the CDs in this study and earlier studies, focusing on LOD and selected linear range. The synthesized CDs exhibit behaviour that is either close to or superior to that of the earlier published studies, indicating their potential uses in environmental systems.

To figure out the specific mechanism of the association between Fe3+ ions and CDs, the fluorescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime, and optical band gap were investigated for CDs and CDs + Fe3+. The FL spectra at
λ
ex
= 380 nm for CDs and CDs + Fe3+ were recorded. Fig. 10 a illustrates that CDs showed a high FL emission intensity with a prominent peak at 470 nm, whereas the FL intensity drastically declined when Fe3+ was added. This indicates that the FL intensity of the CDs was dramatically reduced by the binding of Fe3+ ions. The stronger affinity of Fe3+ for –OH groups on the CDs may have played a role in this quenching by modifying the structure of the CDs and diminishing their fluorescence [20]. Additionally, the electrons in the highly excited state of the CDs are forced into the partially filled 3d orbital of Fe3+. This prevents the electrons from returning to the ground state, which leads to the nonradiative electron transfer that involves the partial transfer of the electrons to the d orbital of Fe3+ and contributes to FL quenching [45]. Therefore, the enhancement of the UV–Vis absorbance due to the addition of Fe3+ is confirmed by quenching the FL of CDs.

Fluorescence lifetime measurement is necessary, as it provides the most accurate and practical method to differentiate between static and dynamic quenching. The FL lifetime (
τ
avg
) at
λ
ex
= 380 nm and
λ
em
= 470 nm was evaluated in order to distinguish between dynamic and static quenching of the fluorescence. Fig. 10b depicts the FL decay of the CDs and CDs + Fe3+ with the fitting curve. The estimated
τ
avg
for CDs and CDs + Fe3+ was 5.341 ns and 6.252 ns, respectively. The decay process with Fe3+ showed an increase in the decay components, which confirmed the drop in FL intensity [57]. Moreover, a slight increase in the FL lifetime in the case of CDs + Fe3+ indicates that Fe3+ was successfully combined with CDs to form a complex [58]. Based on this result, CDs + Fe3+ produces non-fluorescent ground state complexes, and it is demonstrated that the FL quenching caused by Fe3+ is static [59].

The optical band gap (
E g
) was determined from the absorption spectrum using Tauc’s method, which involves plotting
( α h ν )
2
versus energy (
h ν
) and drawing the tangent line where
( α h ν )
2
= 0
to obtain
E g
[60]. As displayed in Fig. 10c, the
E g
was 3.40 eV (inset figure) and 3.15 eV for CDs and CDs + Fe3+, respectively. Kamali et al. found that the presence of Cr(III) lowered the
E g
of the CDs [61], and the metal ion charges on the surface of CDs were linked to this effect [62]. The decrease in
E g
of CDs following the addition of Fe3+ signifies changes in their electronic structure and energy levels due to interaction between Fe3+ ions and CDs. Fe3+ ions have the ability to interact with the functional groups (such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amine) on the surface of CDs. This interaction causes electron transfer or coordination, where Fe3+ ions serve as electron acceptors. This suggests that the interaction of CDs with Fe3+ ions may alter their electronic properties, leading to the formation of new intermediate states between the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) which reduce the effective
E g
of CDs. These new states arise from complexation of Fe3+ with the surface functional groups and disturbance in the π-conjugated system of the CDs. As a result, the energy difference between the VB and CB (i.e., the band gap) reduces.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ was measured and compared with the FL spectra of CDs. FeCl3 typically exhibits absorption in UV and Vis region (250–500 nm) due to ligand-to-metal charge transfer and potential d-d electronic transitions of Fe3+ ions. In addition, a strong absorption band at ∼270–350 nm, corresponds to ligand-to-metal charge transfer transitions, where ligands transfer electrons to Fe3+ ions. The FL quenching occurs through the inner filter effect (IFE) when the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ overlaps with the excitation/emission FL spectra of the CDs [11]. In Fig. 10d, it is observed that the absorption band of Fe3+ in the wavelength range of 260–500 nm had a large part overlap with the excitation peak of CDs at 378 nm and a small part overlap with the emission peak at 472 nm [46]. This type of spectral overlap suggested that the FL of CDs might be quenched by Fe3+ due to IFE. Further, it was found from the FL lifetime that there is a negligible change after the interaction with Fe3+. Therefore, the absorbance enhancement can be a result of IFE. This implies that the FL released by excited CDs might be absorbed by Fe3+ which is a typical sign of the IFE, thereby causing the emission intensity of the CDs to drop (Fig. 10a). Hence, it is suggested that the FL of the CDs was lowered by Fe3+ through the IFE process [53]. The combined effects of surface complexation, electron transfer, and internal filtration explain the observed changes in the absorbance spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The capability of the synthesized CDs to detect Fe3+ ions in actual environmental samples was tested. Three water samples from the lake, farm, and tap were collected and filtered using a 0.22 µm syringe filter. Each water sample was spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (5, 10, 15, and 50 μM). Prior to measuring the absorbance, the solutions were vortexed and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. Each experiment was carried out three times. As seen in Fig. 11 a, the CDs dispersion showed well-absorbed responses to Fe3+ ions. It is evident from Fig. 11b-d that the absorbance of the CDs increased with the rising concentration of Fe3+ ions in lake, farm, and tap water samples. A strong linear correlation and high
R
2
with recoveries for Fe3+ in the range of 95.7–113.0 % (n = 3) were obtained. These outcomes proved the applicability of the suggested method for detection of Fe3+ ions in actual water.

This study promotes sustainability and innovative advancements in nanotechnology by highlighting the potential of biomass-derived nanomaterials for environmental monitoring. Polyalthia bullata root extract serves as an ecofriendly carbon precursor, addressing the growing demand for greener alternatives in material production. The synthesis of the CDs from biomass of the root extract of Polyalthia bullata Knig as a precursor using a simple hydrothermal process was successfully performed. The characteristics of the CDs, including particle size, fluorescence, UV–Vis absorbance, XPS, FTIR, and XRD were carried out. The synthesized CDs have a blue fluorescence, strong stability, and excellent water solubility. TEM images revealed an average diameter of ∼3.34 ± 0.76 nm with a spherical shape. The FTIR and XPS showed the existence of various functional groups on the surface of the CDs which are responsible for their good dispersibility and solubility in water. The evaluation of the interaction between the CD with different metal ions revealed that they are highly selective to Fe3+ ions and thus can be utilized as a colorimetric sensor. Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 57 µM showed an excellent linear relationship with
R
2
of 0.9926 and a limit of detection of 0.186 µM. Further, the effectiveness of the proposed method was assessed by identifying the Fe3+ levels in real water samples such as lake, farm, and tap water with acceptable recovery rates. The current study highlights the sustainable utilization of biomass waste and advances the practicality of nanomaterials for environmental applications.

While the synthesized CDs showed excellent sensitivity with a low LOD of 0.186 µM and practical applicability in real water samples, challenges like scalability and long-term stability in complex matrices persist. The scale-up potential of the hydrothermal process remains unclear, particularly concerning the consistency of the CDs properties and the economic feasibility for industrial production. Likewise, the optimization of detection properties is limited by the ongoing research into the exact mechanisms and interactions between CDs and heavy metals. Future development directions should focus on enhancing optical characteristics, exploring broader analyte detection, and integrating CDs into portable and affordable sensing devices for widespread environmental and industrial monitoring. Further, the potential of these nanomaterials can be unlocked by investigating other applications beyond ion detection, such as in bioimaging, drug delivery, or photocatalysis.

Muna E. Raypah: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mohd Faizal Jamlos: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hadiza Shehu Giwa: Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hajar Fauzan Ahmad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Hazrulrizawati Abd Hamid: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Muna E. Raypah received the B.Sc. degree (Hons.) in Physics from Hodeida University, Al-Hodeida, Yemen, in 2003, and the M.Sc. degree (Hons.) in Solid-State Physics from the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia, in 2013. She was a Graduate Assistant at the Universiti of Science Malaysia from October 2014 to May 2015. From June 2015 to May 2018, she received a Ph.D. Fellowship Award from the Universiti of Science Malaysia and completed her Ph.D. degree (Hons.) in thermal processes in devices and packaging from the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia, in 2020. From July 2021 to June 2023, she served as a Postdoctoral Fellow in the School of Physics, Universiti of Science Malaysia, Penang, Malaysia. Since August 2023, she has been a Postdoctoral Fellow at Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah, Gambang, Malaysia. Her research interests include materials characterization, carbon quantum dots, optical devices, and chemometrics.

Prof. Mohd Faizal Jamlos obtained his Ph.D. in 2010 from Universiti Teknologi Malaysia, Malaysia in Electrical Engineering. He has received the Master of Engineering in Electrical & Electronic in 2008 from University of Adelaide, Australia. Previously, he obtained his first degree from Universiti Malaysia Perlis, Malaysia, with Honours, in Computer Engineering in 2006. He is currently Board of Director, a full professor in Faculty of Electrical & Electronics Engineering Technology and Principal Fellow at the Centre for Automotive Engineering in Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah, Malaysia. He is appointed as visiting scholar of AI in Karlsruhe University of Applied Sciences, Karlsruhe, Germany. More than RM 3 million in total funding by industries, government and university traits his RDICE’s activities. He is active in providing consultation, training and lectures in IoT, AI & convergence technologies and published more than 300 research papers with 30 H-index. His latest focus research area is in convergence technologies of AI, Big Data, Cloud (ABC) + IoT + AR + Digital Twin for various applications include Carbon Quantum Dots-based sensing, Carbon Footprint, Disaster, Aquaculture, Predictive Maintenance and Remote Assistance. He has currently established a start-up, Inference Sdn. Bhd. for commercializing his Water Quality System dan Data Analytics Services. He is an evaluator of varieties research grants include MOSTI and MOHE. He is a Professional Engineer with Practicing Certificate of BEM, Senior Member of IEEE and Corporate Member of Institute Engineers Malaysia (MIEM).

Hadiza Shehu Giwa is a lecturer at the Department of Pure and Applied Chemistry, Kaduna State University, Kaduna, Nigeria. She is currently undergoing her PhD. Program on natural products at the Faculty of Industrial Sciences and Technology, Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah, and her current research covers the use of herbal plant to discover novel drugs with anticancer, antimicrobial and antidiabetic properties.

Dr. Hajar Fauzan Ahmad, Associate Professor Ts. is a dedicated researcher specializing in microbiome studies, antimicrobial resistance (AMR), and next-generation sequencing technologies. Based at Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al Sultan Abdullah, he leads efforts to decode the microbiome, uncovering its critical roles in health, environmental ecosystems, and microbial interactions. Dr. Fauzan’s innovative use of next-generation sequencing has identified pathogenic microbes from wild ecosystems, providing valuable insights into their behavior and potential risks. His current research explores the use of carbon quantum dots derived from natural waste to develop novel antimicrobial strategies against these harmful bacteria.

Dr. Hazrulrizawati Abd Hamid, is an Associate Professor in Industrial Chemistry Department at Faculty of Industrial Sciences & Technology, Universiti Malaysia Pahang Al-Sultan Abdullah. She received her B. Sc. and M.Sc. in Chemistry from Universiti Teknologi Malaysia. She did her doctoral research in Natural Products at Universiti Malaysia Pahang. Previously, she was a chemistry teacher for eight years. She was interested in most branches of chemistry. Her research focuses on chemical analysis of natural products, food, and environmental samples, emphasizing the isolation and characterization of bioactive compounds, chemical compounds profiling, chemometric analysis and method development in spectroscopy and chromatography. Dr. Hazrulrizawati has published extensively in high-impact journals on topics such as antioxidant properties, cytotoxicity, enzyme inhibition, and phenolic profiling. She has also authored award-winning books, including works on the health benefits of natural products. Her dedication to advancing science is reflected in her supervision of numerous PhD and MSc candidates and leadership in research projects exploring drug discovery and food chemistry innovations. In addition to her academic achievements, she actively participates in providing consultancy services, leveraging her expertise in organic spectroscopy and environmental chemistry.
